perturbation.original_text: 4. TERM: This Agreement pertains only to the Confidential Information that is disclosed between the Effective Date (last signature date) and one year from that date. Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for a period of three years from the date the Confidential Information is disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. TERM: This Agreement pertains only to the Confidential Information that is disclosed between the Effective Date (last signature date) and one year from that date. Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for a period of one year from the date the Confidential Information is disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The change in Section 4 alters the duration for which Confidential Information must be maintained in confidence from 'three years' to 'one year'. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 8 and Section 9 where the receiving party has to protect confidential information for longer time. This contradiction introduces uncertainty regarding the enforceable confidentiality period, leading to potential disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information delivered by either Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party. No copies shall be made without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. All Confidential Information including the copies made thereof is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed upon written request by the Disclosing Party. Nothwithstanding the foregoing, a Receiving Party may retain a single archival copy of the received Confidential Information which may be used solely for legal evidentiary purposes in the event of a dispute arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4 reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. This directly contradicts the implication in Section 8 which states that the Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party. Although it does not explicitly state a time frame of when the Disclosing Party can no longer stake a claim on the Confidential Information, it does imply that there are conditions for the protection of that information. This creates an in-text contradiction as per the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition where different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties.

perturbation.original_text: 5. IDENTIFICATION OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information shall be identified prior to disclosure with an appropriate marking or identification such as CONFIDENTIAL or any similar legend. If such information is disclosed either orally or visually, then the Parties will use reasonable efforts to assure protection pursuant to this Agreement. The Parties shall use reasonable effort to reduce such oral or visual Confidential Information to tangible form otherwise in compliance with this Agreement and furnish a copy to the Parties within thirty (30) business days of the original oral or visual disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. IDENTIFICATION OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information shall be identified prior to disclosure with an appropriate marking or identification such as CONFIDENTIAL or any similar legend. If such information is disclosed either orally or visually, then the Parties are not required to assure protection pursuant to this Agreement. The Parties shall use reasonable effort to reduce such oral or visual Confidential Information to tangible form otherwise in compliance with this Agreement and furnish a copy to the Parties within thirty (30) business days of the original oral or visual disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'will use reasonable efforts to assure protection' to 'are not required to assure protection' for orally or visually disclosed information, Section 5 now contradicts Section 9, which mandates a 'reasonable degree of care' for *all* Confidential Information, regardless of disclosure method. It also contradicts section 6 which has a line such as 'The Receiving Party will not attempt to determine the content or structure, or otherwise reverse engineer or decompile any material sample, hardware or software to which it is provided access pursuant to this Agreement except as expressly provided under Section 3 of this Agreement.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. STANDARD OF CARE: The Receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5 removes the requirement to assure protection for orally or visually disclosed information, directly contradicting Section 9 which mandates a 'reasonable degree of care' for *all* Confidential Information, regardless of disclosure method. Therefore, there is a direct conflict, and the change creates a strong contradiction within the document.

perturbation.original_text: 6. NON-DISCLOSURE: The Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party individual, corporation, or other entity without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and shall further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its employees having a "need to know" the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth in this Agreement, and to ensure that such employees are informed of the confidential nature thereof and agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein. The Receiving Party will not attempt to determine the content or structure, or otherwise reverse engineer or decompile any material sample, hardware or software to which it is provided access pursuant to this Agreement except as expressly provided under Section 3 of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. NON-DISCLOSURE: The Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party individual, corporation, or other entity without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and shall further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its employees having a "need to know" the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth in this Agreement, and ensure such employees are informed but are not required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein. The Receiving Party will not attempt to determine the content or structure, or otherwise reverse engineer or decompile any material sample, hardware or software to which it is provided access pursuant to this Agreement except as expressly provided under Section 3 of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'agree to and are required to' in 'ensure that such employees are informed of the confidential nature thereof and agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein' in Section 6, creates a conflict with Section 9, which mandates a 'reasonable degree of care' for all Confidential Information. If employees are not required to observe confidentiality, the 'reasonable degree of care' standard becomes unenforceable. Also, creates contradiction with section 4. TERM: Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for a period of three years from the date the Confidential Information is disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. STANDARD OF CARE: The Receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6 removes the requirement for employees to 'agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality'. This directly contradicts Section 9, which mandates the Receiving Party to protect Confidential Information with a 'reasonable degree of care'. If employees are not required to observe confidentiality, enforcing the 'reasonable degree of care' standard becomes problematic. The change also contradicts section 4, which requires the receiving party to maintain information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this agreement. The original text ensures employees are bound by the confidentiality terms, while the changed text removes this obligation, creating a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 8. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information delivered by either Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party. No copies shall be made without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. All Confidential Information including the copies made thereof is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed upon written request by the Disclosing Party. Nothwithstanding the foregoing, a Receiving Party may retain a single archival copy of the received Confidential Information which may be used solely for legal evidentiary purposes in the event of a dispute arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information delivered by either Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Copies can be made without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. All Confidential Information including the copies made thereof is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed upon written request by the Disclosing Party. Nothwithstanding the foregoing, a Receiving Party may retain a single archival copy of the received Confidential Information which may be used solely for legal evidentiary purposes in the event of a dispute arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change in Section 8, altering 'No copies shall be made without prior written consent' to 'Copies can be made without prior written consent,' creates a contradiction with Section 6. Section 6 restricts disclosure and circulation. If copies can be freely made (per Section 8), it undermines the non-disclosure obligations in Section 6, introducing an in-text conflict. The section 6 'shall further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its employees having a need to know.' is not being held if copies can be freely made.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. NON-DISCLOSURE: The Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party individual, corporation, or other entity without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and shall further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its employees having a "need to know" the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth in this Agreement, and to ensure that such employees are informed of the confidential nature thereof and agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein. The Receiving Party will not attempt to determine the content or structure, or otherwise reverse engineer or decompile any material sample, hardware or software to which it is provided access pursuant to this Agreement except as expressly provided under Section 3 of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that 'No copies shall be made without prior written consent'. The changed text modifies this to 'Copies can be made without prior written consent.' This directly contradicts Section 6, which requires the Receiving Party to 'further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization'. If copies can be made freely, as the changed text indicates, it undermines the restriction on circulation and disclosure outlined in Section 6, creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 9. STANDARD OF CARE: The Receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. STANDARD OF CARE: The Receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a minimal degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable degree of care' to 'minimal degree of care' in Section 9 directly contradicts the intent and enforceability of Section 6. Section 6 has a line such as 'and to ensure that such employees are informed of the confidential nature thereof and agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein.' requiring a higher level of confidentiality protection. This now means that the employees of the Receiving party don't need to be so careful with Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. NON-DISCLOSURE: The Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party individual, corporation, or other entity without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and shall further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its employees having a "need to know" the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth in this Agreement, and to ensure that such employees are informed of the confidential nature thereof and agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein. The Receiving Party will not attempt to determine the content or structure, or otherwise reverse engineer or decompile any material sample, hardware or software to which it is provided access pursuant to this Agreement except as expressly provided under Section 3 of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable degree of care' to 'minimal degree of care' in Section 9 directly contradicts the requirement in Section 6 that employees 'are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein'. Requiring a 'minimal' degree of care undermines the explicit requirement of employees upholding confidentiality, creating a conflict about the level of protection expected.

perturbation.original_text: 14. LIMITED WARRANTY AND LIABILITY: Discloser warrants that it has the right to disclose Confidential Information to Recipient. Discloser makes no other warranties with respect to the Confidential Information and provides all information "AS IS" without any express or implied warranty of any kind, including any warranty as to merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, accuracy, completeness or violation of third party intellectual property rights. Neither Party will be liable for any special, incidental or consequential damages of any kind whatsoever resulting from the disclosure, use or receipt of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. LIMITED WARRANTY AND LIABILITY: Discloser warrants that it has the right to disclose Confidential Information to Recipient. Discloser makes other warranties with respect to the Confidential Information and provides all information with any express or implied warranty of any kind, including any warranty as to merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, accuracy, completeness or violation of third party intellectual property rights. Neither Party will be liable for any special, incidental or consequential damages of any kind whatsoever resulting from the disclosure, use or receipt of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The change in Section 14, from 'Discloser makes no other warranties' to 'Discloser makes other warranties' and from 'without any express or implied warranty' to 'with any express or implied warranty', now contradicts the 'AS IS' condition. This contradiction creates an ambiguity in the liability and responsibilities of the Discloser related to the confidential information, potentially creating conflicts with Section 9, which dictates the 'standard of care' where the Receiving party has to treat the disclosed information with same degree of care, but no less that a reasonable degree of care
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. STANDARD OF CARE: The Receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the Discloser makes no other warranties and provides information "AS IS" without any express or implied warranty. The changed text reverses this, stating the Discloser makes other warranties and provides information with express or implied warranties. This directly contradicts Section 9, which requires the Receiving Party to protect confidential information with a 'reasonable degree of care.' The 'AS IS' condition in the original text limits the Discloser's liability, while the changed text expands it by introducing warranties. This expansion creates a strong contradiction, potentially altering the standard of care expected from the Receiving Party, as the Discloser is now warranting the information. Because of the strong incompatibility, the answer is YES.
